talks.title: The Art (and some Science) of Influential Communication
talks.speaker: Matt Sodnicar

questions.question: When a conversation at work breaks down, what is the most common root cause?
options: One person has bad intentions
People process and organize information differently — your message may not land the way you sent it
Technical jargon is used incorrectly
The listener isn't paying attention

questions.question: Recognizing how someone else processes information lets you do what?
options: Predict whether they will agree with you before speaking
Adapt how you frame and sequence your message so it maps to their mental model
Simplify your message to its lowest common denominator
Speak more slowly and use bullet points

questions.question: The frustration cycle in workplace communication — where both parties feel unheard — is best broken by doing what first?
options: Repeating your message more loudly or in writing
Asking clarifying questions to identify the other person's information-processing style before re-framing your message
Escalating to a shared manager to mediate
Switching to asynchronous communication to remove emotional pressure

questions.question: Norbert Wiener coined the term "cybernetics" to describe the study of what?
options: Electronic computing hardware
Control and communication in animals and machines
The mathematics of information and entropy
Autonomous robotic systems

questions.question: What technology does Justin Fritz use in his Robo Norbert Wiener talk to explore the state of AI?
options: A physical robot modeled on Wiener's body measurements
An AI language model trained to simulate Wiener and explain his own legacy
A 1950s-era analog computer restored to working order
Archival voice recordings of Wiener synthesized into a chatbot

questions.question: What Wiener prediction from the 1950s is most relevant to the current wave of large language models and AI automation?
options: That computers would eventually surpass human chess players
That the internet would connect all humans into a single information network
That automation would displace human labor and concentrate economic power unless society proactively adapted its institutions
That machines would develop emotions and require legal rights

originStory.text:
Hi. I'm Justin Fritz — and I'm a Nerd Nite Boss.

That's a real title. The Nerd Nite organization gave it to me, and I take it very seriously.

Nerd Nite started in Boston in 2003. Graduate student Chris Balakrishnan had been telling the same stories at his local bar — The Midway Cafe — about studying parasitic birds in Cameroon. The bartenders finally told him: just give a talk. One night. Slides. Get it out of your system.

He did. It worked.

In 2006, his roommate Matt Wasowski moved the concept to New York City, expanded it beyond science to any topic worth a drink and 20 minutes, and built it into a global brand — the "Discovery Channel with beer" — now in over 100 cities worldwide.

It came to Fort Collins because of me and one other very important person: Senne Van Loon.

But first — trivia.

Senne and I built this thing from scratch.

Senne is the kind of person who hears "let's put on a nerdy event at a bar" and says yes immediately — full commitment, no hesitation. That energy is rare and it matters. You cannot build something like this alone.

As we grew, Jamie Fritz joined us. Jamie is the backbone — always behind the scenes, making everything work, never needing the spotlight. The kind of person every organization needs and rarely thanks enough.

Jamie has been onstage exactly once: to introduce Marybeth Arcodia. Which brings me to Marybeth.

Marybeth Arcodia was our host.

She was brilliant at it — warm, sharp, funny in exactly the right way for a room full of nerds on their third drink. She held the stage like she was born for it.

Marybeth eventually left Fort Collins for a faculty position in Miami. We were sad to lose her and enormously proud of her.

Hannah Bauer stepped up as host after Marybeth — and she's been incredible.

Here's the thing you need to understand about Nerd Nite: once you're a Boss, you're always a Boss. Marybeth is still a Boss. Distance doesn't change that.

You just played Nerdometer.

Every edition is built around a real Nerd Nite Fort Collins event — the actual talks, the actual speakers, the real nerdy obsessions people bring to a bar and share with strangers.

Senne. Jamie. Marybeth. Hannah. Every speaker who ever trusted a room full of people with the thing they love most.

All Bosses. Always.

Come be part of it.

— Justin Fritz, Nerd Nite Fort Collins Boss
